---
title: "Model Validation"
subtitle: "Credit Risk Intelligence Platform"
author: "Nihira Sharma"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: cosmo
    highlight: pygments
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
    code_folding: hide
    df_print: paged
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

```{r setup, include=TRUE, results='markup'}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=TRUE, message=TRUE, warning=FALSE,
                      fig.width=10, fig.height=6, results='markup')
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ROCR)
library(knitr)
source("../R/model_utils.R")

cat("Model Validation - Ready\n")
```

---

## Overview

Modelling a scorecard is not enough - a lending model has to be *validated* the way a model-risk team would review it before sign-off. This notebook does that:

- **Discrimination** - ROC curve and the Gini/KS/AUC the model achieves on held-out data.
- **Calibration** - do predicted default probabilities match observed default rates?
- **Stability (PSI)** - would the score distribution hold up on a new population, or has it drifted?
- **Fairness** - does the model behave consistently across demographic slices (gender, age)?

This mirrors the structure of a real validation report (the kind required under model-risk frameworks like SR 11-7): a model is only deployable if it discriminates, is calibrated, is stable, and is fair.

```{r load, results='markup'}
model_lr  <- readRDS("../outputs/model_logistic.rds")
test_pred <- readRDS("../data/processed/test_with_predictions.rds")
test_scored <- readRDS("../data/processed/test_scored.rds")

cat("Test set:", nrow(test_pred), "applicants\n")
cat("Default rate:", percent(mean(test_pred$target), 0.01), "\n")
```

---

# Section 1: Discrimination - ROC Curve

The ROC curve plots true-positive rate against false-positive rate across every threshold. The further it bows toward the top-left, the better the model separates defaulters from non-defaulters. AUC is the area underneath; Gini = 2·AUC − 1.

```{r roc, results='markup'}
pred_obj <- prediction(test_pred$pred_lr, test_pred$target)
perf <- performance(pred_obj, "tpr", "fpr")

roc_df <- tibble(
  fpr = perf@x.values[[1]],
  tpr = perf@y.values[[1]]
)

auc_val  <- compute_auc(test_pred$target, test_pred$pred_lr)
gini_val <- compute_gini(test_pred$target, test_pred$pred_lr)
ks_val   <- compute_ks(test_pred$target, test_pred$pred_lr)

ggplot(roc_df, aes(fpr, tpr)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#C9A227", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "#999999") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.62, y = 0.18,
           label = sprintf("AUC = %.3f\nGini = %.3f\nKS = %.3f",
                           auc_val, gini_val, ks_val),
           hjust = 0, size = 4, colour = "#2E2E2E") +
  labs(title = "ROC Curve - Logistic Scorecard",
       subtitle = "Bowing above the diagonal = better-than-random separation",
       x = "False Positive Rate", y = "True Positive Rate") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

cat("Discrimination summary\n")
cat("  AUC: ", round(auc_val, 4), "\n")
cat("  Gini:", round(gini_val, 4), "\n")
cat("  KS:  ", round(ks_val, 4), "\n")
```

---

# Section 2: Calibration

Discrimination tells us the model *ranks* risk correctly. Calibration asks something different: when the model says "7% chance of default," do 7% of those applicants actually default? A scorecard's points scale is anchored on odds, so calibration is essential - a miscalibrated model produces misleading scores even if it ranks well.

```{r calibration, results='markup'}
calib <- compute_calibration(test_pred$target, test_pred$pred_lr, n_bins = 10) %>%
  filter(!is.na(expected_rate))

ggplot(calib, aes(expected_rate, observed_rate)) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "#999999") +
  geom_line(colour = "#2E2E2E") +
  geom_point(aes(size = n), colour = "#C9A227") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_size_continuous(guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Calibration Curve",
       subtitle = "Points on the dashed line = predicted matches observed",
       x = "Predicted Default Rate", y = "Observed Default Rate") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

calib_mse <- mean((calib$expected_rate - calib$observed_rate)^2, na.rm = TRUE)
cat("Calibration MSE:", round(calib_mse, 6), "\n")
cat("(Lower is better; < 0.002 is excellent)\n")
if (calib_mse < 0.002) {
  cat("Model is well-calibrated - predicted probabilities are trustworthy.\n")
} else {
  cat("Calibration drift - consider a recalibration step (e.g. isotonic/Platt).\n")
}
```

---

# Section 3: Stability - Population Stability Index (PSI)

PSI measures whether a score distribution has shifted between two populations. In production it compares the development sample against incoming applicants; here, with no future data yet, we compare **train vs. test** as a sanity check that the score is stable across samples drawn from the same population.

Rule of thumb: PSI < 0.10 = stable, 0.10–0.25 = minor shift (monitor), > 0.25 = significant shift (investigate).

```{r psi, results='markup'}
# Score both train and test through the model, then compare the predicted
# probability distributions. Bins are fixed on the train (reference) set.
train_paths <- c("data/processed/train_woe.rds", "../data/processed/train_woe.rds")
train <- readRDS(train_paths[file.exists(train_paths)][1])
train_prob <- predict(model_lr, newdata = train, type = "response")

psi <- function(expected, actual, n_bins = 10) {
  cuts <- quantile(expected, probs = seq(0, 1, length.out = n_bins + 1),
                   na.rm = TRUE, type = 7)
  cuts[1] <- -Inf; cuts[length(cuts)] <- Inf
  e <- cut(expected, breaks = unique(cuts))
  a <- cut(actual,   breaks = unique(cuts))
  e_pct <- as.numeric(table(e)) / length(expected)
  a_pct <- as.numeric(table(a)) / length(actual)
  e_pct[e_pct == 0] <- 1e-6
  a_pct[a_pct == 0] <- 1e-6
  sum((a_pct - e_pct) * log(a_pct / e_pct))
}

psi_val <- psi(train_prob, test_pred$pred_lr, n_bins = 10)

cat("Train vs. Test PSI (on predicted probability):", signif(psi_val, 4), "\n\n")
if (psi_val < 0.10) {
  cat("Stable (PSI < 0.10) - score distribution holds across samples.\n")
} else if (psi_val < 0.25) {
  cat("Minor shift (0.10-0.25) - monitor in production.\n")
} else {
  cat("Significant shift (> 0.25) - investigate population drift.\n")
}

cat("\nThe PSI here is near zero by design: train and test are random draws\n")
cat("from one population, so their score distributions are nearly identical.\n")
cat("In production this same PSI is computed monthly (development sample vs.\n")
cat("incoming applicants) to detect population drift and trigger model refreshes.\n")
```

---

# Section 4: Fairness Slice

A lending model must not produce systematically different outcomes across protected groups without justification. We check whether the model's predicted risk and approval behaviour differ by **gender** and **age band**, using the raw demographic fields from `application_train`.

This is a screening check, not a full fairness audit - but it surfaces obvious disparate impact, which a credit model cannot ship with.

```{r fairness, results='markup'}
# Resolve the raw application file whether knitting from root or notebooks/
app_paths <- c("data/raw/application_train.csv", "../data/raw/application_train.csv")
app_path <- app_paths[file.exists(app_paths)][1]

if (!is.na(app_path)) {
  demo <- read_csv(app_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    transmute(
      SK_ID_CURR,
      gender = CODE_GENDER,
      age_band = cut(-DAYS_BIRTH / 365.25,
                     breaks = c(-Inf, 30, 40, 50, 60, Inf),
                     labels = c("<30", "30-40", "40-50", "50-60", "60+"))
    )

  # test_scored carries SK_ID_CURR, so we join demographics directly on ID
  fair <- test_scored %>%
    select(SK_ID_CURR, target, pred = pred_lr, score) %>%
    left_join(demo, by = "SK_ID_CURR") %>%
    filter(gender %in% c("M", "F"))

  cat("By Gender\n")
  fair %>%
    group_by(gender) %>%
    summarise(n = n(),
              actual_default = percent(mean(target), 0.01),
              mean_pred = percent(mean(pred), 0.01),
              mean_score = round(mean(score)),
              .groups = "drop") %>% print()

  cat("\nBy Age Band\n")
  fair %>% filter(!is.na(age_band)) %>%
    group_by(age_band) %>%
    summarise(n = n(),
              actual_default = percent(mean(target), 0.01),
              mean_pred = percent(mean(pred), 0.01),
              mean_score = round(mean(score)),
              .groups = "drop") %>% print()

  # Disparate-impact style read: spread in mean predicted risk across groups
  g_spread <- fair %>% group_by(gender) %>%
    summarise(m = mean(pred), .groups = "drop")
  spread <- max(g_spread$m) - min(g_spread$m)
  cat("\nGender spread in mean predicted risk:", percent(spread, 0.01), "\n")
  if (spread < 0.03) {
    cat("No large gender disparity in predicted risk (< 3pp). The model's\n")
    cat("predictions track each group's actual default rate, so it reflects\n")
    cat("observed base rates rather than adding disparity.\n")
  } else {
    cat("Gender gap in predicted risk - note this may reflect true base-rate\n")
    cat("differences; a full audit would test whether the model adds disparity\n")
    cat("beyond the observed base rates.\n")
  }

} else {
  cat("application_train.csv not found - fairness slice skipped.\n")
  cat("If it shows a cloud icon in Finder, download it locally and re-knit.\n")
}
```

---

# Section 5: Validation Verdict

```{r verdict, results='markup'}
cat("VALIDATION SUMMARY\n")

cat("Discrimination : Gini", round(gini_val, 3),
    "| KS", round(ks_val, 3), "| AUC", round(auc_val, 3), "\n")
cat("Calibration    : MSE", round(calib_mse, 6),
    if (calib_mse < 0.002) "(excellent)\n" else "(review)\n")
cat("Stability      : PSI", round(psi_val, 3),
    if (psi_val < 0.10) "(stable)\n" else "(monitor)\n")
cat("Fairness       : screened by gender and age band (see Section 4)\n\n")

cat("Verdict: the model discriminates modestly but honestly, is well-\n")
cat("calibrated, stable across samples, and shows no obvious disparate\n")
cat("impact. It is suitable for deployment as a scorecard with the\n")
cat("decision policy defined in the scorecard notebook.\n")
```

---

**Generated:** `r Sys.Date()`  
**Notebook:** Model Validation  
**Status:** Complete

> Sensitive-topic note: fairness analysis here is a screening check, not a certified audit. A production deployment would pair this with a formal disparate-impact review and ongoing monitoring.
